content_markdown:
An ideal gas is maintained at a temperature of $100\text{ K}$. The variation of the pressure $P$ and $\frac{1}{\text{volume}}$ of the gas is shown.

What is the quantity of the gas?

diagram_metadata.description: A linear graph passing through the origin showing Pressure P in kPa on the y-axis and 1/volume in m^-3 on the x-axis. The line passes through the point (70, 560) approximately, or more clearly (50, 400).

vibe_explanation: Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, we can rewrite it as $P = nRT \left(\frac{1}{V}\right)$. This means the gradient of the $P$ vs $1/V$ graph is equal to $nRT$. From the graph, the gradient is $\frac{400 \text{ kPa}}{50 \text{ m}^{-3}} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{50} = 8000 \text{ Pa m}^3$. Setting $nRT = 8000$ with $T = 100\text{ K}$, we get $n \cdot R \cdot 100 = 8000$, which simplifies to $n = \frac{80}{R}$.
